Now I have all the information needed. Here's the comparison:

## Endpoint Comparison: Documentation vs Implementation

| Endpoint | HTTP Method | Documentation Status | Implementation Status |
|----------|-------------|---------------------|----------------------|
| `/api/users` | GET | ✅ Documented | ✅ Implemented (UsersController.scala - GET case) |
| `/api/users` | POST | ✅ Documented | ✅ Implemented (UsersController.scala - POST case) |
| `/api/health` | GET | ✅ Documented | ✅ Implemented (Main.scala - inline handler) |

### Summary

**All documented endpoints are implemented:**

1. **`GET /api/users`** — Returns a JSON array of all users. The handler is in `UsersController.scala` which matches on the GET method and returns user data.

2. **`POST /api/users`** — Creates a new user. The handler is in `UsersController.scala` which matches on the POST method. Note: The implementation is minimal (just returns `{"status":"created"}`) - the actual body parsing is noted as a TODO.

3. **`GET /api/health`** — Health check endpoint. Implemented inline in `Main.scala` as `_ => Response(200, "OK")`.

### Additional Implementation Details

- The `UsersController.handle` function also handles invalid HTTP methods (returns 405 Method Not Allowed for any method other than GET or POST)
- No additional undocumented endpoints exist beyond what's documented in the README
